question_text: In Neyman-Pearson decision theory, define \(H_0\), \(H_1\), the critical region, Type I error, Type II error, \(\alpha\), \(\beta\), and power.
solution_text: \(H_0\) is the null hypothesis and \(H_1\) is the alternative hypothesis. A test statistic is computed from the data, and the critical region is the set of statistic values for which the rule says reject \(H_0\). A Type I error is rejecting \(H_0\) when \(H_0\) is true; \(\alpha=P(\text{reject }H_0\mid H_0\text{ true})\). A Type II error is failing to reject \(H_0\) when \(H_1\) is true; \(\beta=P(\text{fail to reject }H_0\mid H_1\text{ true})\). Power is \(1-\beta=P(\text{reject }H_0\mid H_1\text{ true})\).
thought_text: Always condition the error probability on which hypothesis or parameter value is true.

question_text: A medical screening rule rejects \(H_0:\) patient is disease-free when the test statistic exceeds a cutoff. The alternative is \(H_1:\) patient has the disease. Describe the Type I and Type II errors in words.
solution_text: A Type I error is rejecting \(H_0\) when the patient is actually disease-free. In words, this is a false positive: the rule says the patient has the disease when they do not. A Type II error is failing to reject \(H_0\) when the patient actually has the disease. In words, this is a false negative: the screening rule misses a diseased patient.
thought_text: Do not decide which error is more serious until the context is specified; screening and confirmatory testing often value the errors differently.

source: exercises_ch17.html | Original. Direct normal critical-region computation.
question_text: Let \(X_1,\ldots,X_{16}\sim N(\mu,4^2)\). Test \(H_0:\mu=50\) against \(H_1:\mu>50\) with the rule reject \(H_0\) if \(\bar X>52\). Compute \(\alpha\).
solution_text: Under \(H_0\), \(\bar X\sim N(50,4^2/16)\), so the standard error is \(1\). Therefore \[\alpha=P_{50}(\bar X>52)=P\left(Z>\frac{52-50}{1}\right)=P(Z>2)=0.0228.\]
thought_text: The level is computed assuming the null parameter value, not using the observed sample mean.

question_text: Use the same test as Q3: \(n=16\), \(\sigma=4\), reject \(H_0:\mu=50\) if \(\bar X>52\). Compute \(\beta\) and power when the true mean is \(\mu=53\).
solution_text: When \(\mu=53\), \(\bar X\sim N(53,1^2)\). The Type II error probability at \(\mu=53\) is \[\beta(53)=P_{53}(\bar X\le52)=P\left(Z\le\frac{52-53}{1}\right)=P(Z\le-1)=0.1587.\] The power at \(\mu=53\) is \(1-\beta(53)=0.8413\).
thought_text: For a fixed decision rule, (alpha) is one number here, but ( eta(mu)) changes with the true alternative mean.

source: exercises_ch17.html | Original. Design a one-sided Z test.
question_text: For \(X_1,\ldots,X_{25}\sim N(\mu,3^2)\), design a level \(\alpha=0.05\) test of \(H_0:\mu=100\) against \(H_1:\mu>100\). Give the critical region in terms of \(\bar X\).
solution_text: For an upper-sided test, reject for large \(\bar X\). Under \(H_0\), \(\bar X\sim N(100,(3/\sqrt{25})^2)\), with standard error \(0.6\). Use \(z_{0.05}=1.645\). The cutoff is \(100+1.645(0.6)=100.987\). Thus reject \(H_0\) when \(\bar X>100.987\).
thought_text: The alternative decides the tail direction; the significance level decides the tail area.

question_text: For \(X_1,\ldots,X_{36}\sim N(\mu,6^2)\), design a level \(\alpha=0.10\) two-sided test of \(H_0:\mu=20\) against \(H_1:\mu\ne20\). Give the critical region in terms of \(\bar X\).
solution_text: Under \(H_0\), \(\bar X\sim N(20,(6/\sqrt{36})^2)=N(20,1^2)\). A two-sided \(\alpha=0.10\) test puts \(0.05\) in each tail, so the critical value is \(z_{0.05}=1.645\). Reject \(H_0\) if \(|\bar X-20|>1.645(1)\), equivalently if \(\bar X21.645\).
thought_text: A 10% two-sided test uses the same normal cutoff as a 90% confidence interval.

question_text: A student says, "The \(p\)-value is 0.03, so \(H_0\) is false with probability 0.97." Explain why this is not a Neyman-Pearson or Fisher interpretation.
solution_text: The statement is wrong because the usual frequentist framework treats \(H_0\) as a fixed claim, not as a random event with posterior probability. A \(p\)-value of 0.03 means that, if \(H_0\) were true and the model assumptions held, data at least as incompatible with \(H_0\) would occur with probability 0.03. In Neyman-Pearson testing, a pre-specified rule compares the statistic or \(p\)-value to \(\alpha\) and controls long-run Type I error. In Fisher testing, the \(p\)-value is evidence against \(H_0\), not the probability that \(H_0\) is false.
thought_text: A (p)-value is computed under (H_0); it is not (P(H_0mid ext{data})).

question_text: A 95% two-sided confidence interval for a mean is \([4.2,7.8]\). Without recomputing a test statistic, what decision is made for \(H_0:\mu=8.0\) versus \(H_1:\mu\ne8.0\) at \(\alpha=0.05\)? What about \(H_0:\mu=6.0\)?
solution_text: For the two-sided test at \(\alpha=0.05\), reject a null value exactly when that value is outside the corresponding 95% confidence interval. Since \(8.0\notin[4.2,7.8]\), reject \(H_0:\mu=8.0\). Since \(6.0\in[4.2,7.8]\), fail to reject \(H_0:\mu=6.0\).
thought_text: Duality applies when the confidence level and test significance level match and both are two-sided.

question_text: Let \(X_1,\ldots,X_{64}\sim N(\mu,8^2)\). Test \(H_0:\mu=40\) against \(H_1:\mu<40\) at level \(0.01\). Find the rejection rule in terms of \(\bar X\).
solution_text: Under \(H_0\), the standard error is \(8/\sqrt{64}=1\). A lower-tail level \(0.01\) test rejects for small \(\bar X\). The lower 1% standard normal cutoff is \(-2.326\). So reject \(H_0\) when \((\bar X-40)/1<-2.326\), equivalently when \(\bar X<37.674\).
thought_text: If the alternative is (<), the critical region is in the left tail.

question_text: For \(X_1,\ldots,X_{25}\sim N(\mu,5^2)\), test \(H_0:\mu=10\) against \(H_1:\mu\ne10\) at \(\alpha=0.05\). Compute \(\beta(12)\) and power at \(\mu=12\).
solution_text: The standard error is \(5/\sqrt{25}=1\). The two-sided level 0.05 critical region is \(\bar X10+1.96=11.96\). At \(\mu=12\), \(\bar X\sim N(12,1^2)\). Failure to reject means \(8.04\le\bar X\le11.96\), so \[\beta(12)=P(-3.96\le Z\le -0.04)=\Phi(-0.04)-\Phi(-3.96)\approx0.4840-0.0000=0.4840.\] Power is \(1-\beta(12)\approx0.5160\).
thought_text: For a two-sided test, ( eta) is the probability of landing between two cutoffs under the alternative.

question_text: A test has operating characteristic function \(L(\mu)=P_\mu(\text{fail to reject }H_0)\). Suppose \(H_0:\mu=0\), \(H_1:\mu>0\), and \(L(0)=0.95\), \(L(1)=0.62\), \(L(2)=0.18\). Identify \(\alpha\), \(\beta(1)\), \(\beta(2)\), and the powers at \(\mu=1,2\).
solution_text: The OC function gives the probability of accepting or failing to reject \(H_0\). At the null value, \(L(0)=1-\alpha\), so \(\alpha=1-0.95=0.05\). For alternative values, \(L(\mu)=\beta(\mu)\). Thus \(\beta(1)=0.62\), \(\beta(2)=0.18\), power at \(\mu=1\) is \(0.38\), and power at \(\mu=2\) is \(0.82\).
thought_text: OC curves usually decrease as the true value moves deeper into the rejection-favored alternative.

question_text: Plan a one-sided level \(0.05\) test of \(H_0:\mu=100\) against \(H_1:\mu>100\), with known \(\sigma=10\). Choose \(n\) so that power is at least \(0.90\) when \(\mu=105\).
solution_text: The level \(0.05\) upper-tail rule rejects when \(\bar X>100+z_{0.05}\sigma/\sqrt n\), with \(z_{0.05}=1.645\). At \(\mu=105\), requiring power \(0.90\) means \(\beta(105)\le0.10\): \[P_{105}\left(\bar X\le100+1.645\frac{10}{\sqrt n}\right)\le0.10.\] Standardizing gives \[\frac{100+1.645(10/\sqrt n)-105}{10/\sqrt n}\le -1.282.\] Thus \(5\sqrt n/10\ge1.645+1.282=2.927\), so \(\sqrt n\ge5.854\) and \(n\ge34.27\). Round up to \(n=35\).
thought_text: Power planning almost always combines the Type I cutoff and the desired Type II cutoff.

question_text: State the Neyman-Pearson lemma for testing a simple null \(H_0:\theta=\theta_0\) against a simple alternative \(H_1:\theta=\theta_1\). What form does the most powerful level-\(\alpha\) test take?
solution_text: For testing simple \(H_0\) against simple \(H_1\), the Neyman-Pearson lemma says that among all tests with Type I error probability at most \(\alpha\), the likelihood-ratio test is most powerful for detecting \(\theta_1\). Equivalently, reject \(H_0\) for data values where the likelihood under \(H_1\) is sufficiently large relative to the likelihood under \(H_0\): \[\frac{L(\theta_1;x)}{L(\theta_0;x)}>k,\] with \(k\) chosen so that \(P_{\theta_0}(\text{reject})=\alpha\) when possible. Some conventions use \(L(\theta_0;x)/L(\theta_1;x)<c\), which is the same rule.
thought_text: The lemma is about maximizing power at a specified alternative, not about estimating the parameter.

question_text: Classify each pair as simple or composite, and say whether \(\beta\) is a single number or a function over alternatives. \(H_0:\mu=0\), \(H_1:\mu=1\). \(H_0:\mu=0\), \(H_1:\mu>0\). \(H_0:\mu\le0\), \(H_1:\mu>0\).
solution_text: (i) Both null and alternative are simple. For a fixed test, \(\beta\) at \(\mu=1\) is a single number. (ii) The null is simple and the alternative is composite. \(\beta(\mu)\) is a function for \(\mu>0\). (iii) Both hypotheses are composite. The Type I error may need control over all \(\mu\le0\), and \(\beta(\mu)\) is a function over \(\mu>0\).
thought_text: A hypothesis is simple only if it pins down the full parameter value needed to determine the sampling distribution.

source: exercises_ch17.html | Original. Discrete critical region issue.
question_text: A discrete test statistic \(T\) has, under \(H_0\), \(P(T\ge5)=0.031\) and \(P(T\ge4)=0.083\). You want a level \(0.05\) upper-tail test. What non-randomized critical region is available? If exact size 0.05 is required, what idea is needed?
solution_text: The largest non-randomized upper-tail critical region with size at most 0.05 is \(T\ge5\), because its Type I error probability is \(0.031\). Using \(T\ge4\) would exceed the level, since \(0.083>0.05\). If an exact size \(0.05\) test is required, randomization at the boundary \(T=4\) is needed: always reject for \(T\ge5\), reject with some probability \(\gamma\) when \(T=4\), and choose \(\gamma\) so the total null rejection probability is \(0.05\).
thought_text: In discrete problems, exact (alpha) is often impossible without randomized tests.

question_text: A level \(0.05\) test fails to reject \(H_0\). A student concludes, "The probability that \(H_0\) is true is at least 0.95." Diagnose the statement and give a better conclusion.
solution_text: The statement is incorrect. A level \(0.05\) test controls \(P(\text{reject }H_0\mid H_0\text{ true})\), not \(P(H_0\text{ true}\mid\text{data})\). Failing to reject is not the same as proving \(H_0\). A better conclusion is: at the chosen significance level and under the model assumptions, the data do not fall in the pre-specified rejection region, so there is insufficient evidence to reject \(H_0\). This may reflect either a true null or low power.
thought_text: Neyman-Pearson tests make decisions under controlled long-run error rates; they do not assign truth probabilities to hypotheses.

question_text: For a fixed sample size and model, what usually happens to \(\beta\) and power if \(\alpha\) is made smaller? Explain using critical regions.
solution_text: Making \(\alpha\) smaller usually shrinks the critical region, because the test must reject less often under \(H_0\). With a smaller rejection region, it is also harder to reject under alternative parameter values. Therefore \(\beta\), the probability of failing to reject under the alternative, usually increases, and power \(1-\beta\) decreases. The exception would require changing some other design feature, such as increasing \(n\) or changing the test statistic.
thought_text: There is no free reduction of both error probabilities unless information is added, usually through a larger sample size.

question_text: A 95% lower confidence bound for \(\mu\) is \(L=12.1\). Use confidence-bound duality to decide a level \(0.05\) test of \(H_0:\mu\le10\) against \(H_1:\mu>10\).
solution_text: A 95% lower confidence bound corresponds to a level 0.05 upper-sided test. If the lower bound is above the null boundary 10, then all parameter values \(\mu\le10\) are excluded in the direction relevant to \(H_1:\mu>10\). Since \(12.1>10\), reject \(H_0:\mu\le10\) at level 0.05. The data support the claim that \(\mu>10\) under the model assumptions.
thought_text: Use a lower confidence bound for greater-than alternatives and an upper confidence bound for less-than alternatives.

question_text: A factory claims the mean fill is \(\mu=500\) ml. The known standard deviation is \(\sigma=12\) ml. From \(n=36\) bottles, \(\bar x=496.1\). Test \(H_0:\mu=500\) against \(H_1:\mu<500\) at \(\alpha=0.05\).
solution_text: The standard error is \(12/\sqrt{36}=2\). For a lower-tail level 0.05 test, reject if \[Z=\frac{\bar X-500}{2}<-1.645,\] equivalently if \(\bar X<500-1.645(2)=496.71\). Since \(496.1<496.71\), reject \(H_0\). The observed statistic is \(z=(496.1-500)/2=-1.95\), which is in the rejection region.
thought_text: For one-sided tests, do not double the tail area unless the problem asks for a two-sided test.

question_text: A two-sided known-variance test of \(H_0:\mu=80\) uses \(n=49\), \(\sigma=14\), and accepts \(H_0\) when \(76\le\bar X\le84\). What is the significance level \(\alpha\)?
solution_text: Under \(H_0\), \(\bar X\sim N(80,(14/\sqrt{49})^2)=N(80,2^2)\). The acceptance endpoints are 4 units from 80, or \(4/2=2\) standard errors. Thus the rejection region is \(|Z|>2\), and \(\alpha=P(|Z|>2)=2P(Z>2)=2(0.0228)=0.0456.\)
thought_text: The acceptance region is the complement of the critical region.

question_text: Let \(X\sim\operatorname{Binomial}(10,p)\). Use the Neyman-Pearson idea to test \(H_0:p=0.3\) against \(H_1:p=0.6\). Show why the most powerful test rejects for large \(X\), and find the largest critical region of the form \(X\ge c\) with \(\alpha\le0.05\).
solution_text: The likelihood ratio favoring \(H_1\) over \(H_0\) is \[\frac{L(0.6;x)}{L(0.3;x)}=\frac{\binom{10}{x}0.6^x0.4^{10-x}}{\binom{10}{x}0.3^x0.7^{10-x}}=\left(\frac{0.6}{0.3}\right)^x\left(\frac{0.4}{0.7}\right)^{10-x}.\] This increases with \(x\), so the NP most powerful critical region is in the upper tail. Under \(p=0.3\), \(P(X\ge7)=0.0106+0.0014+0.0001+0.000006\approx0.012\), while \(P(X\ge6)\approx0.0473\). Thus \(X\ge6\) is the largest upper-tail critical region with \(\alpha\le0.05\).
thought_text: For binomial simple alternatives, the likelihood ratio is monotone when the alternative success probability is larger.

source: exercises_ch17.html | Original. Derive a power curve.
question_text: For \(X_1,\ldots,X_n\sim N(\mu,\sigma^2)\) with known \(\sigma\), derive the power function for the level-\(\alpha\) test of \(H_0:\mu=\mu_0\) against \(H_1:\mu>\mu_0\).
solution_text: The level-\(\alpha\) upper-tail test rejects when \[\bar X>\mu_0+z_\alpha\frac{\sigma}{\sqrt n}.\] For a general true value \(\mu\), \(\bar X\sim N(\mu,\sigma^2/n)\). Therefore the power function is \[\pi(\mu)=P_\mu\left(\bar X>\mu_0+z_\alpha\frac{\sigma}{\sqrt n}\right)=1-\Phi\left(\frac{\mu_0-\mu}{\sigma/\sqrt n}+z_\alpha\right).\] Equivalently, \(\pi(\mu)=\Phi\left(\frac{\mu-\mu_0}{\sigma/\sqrt n}-z_\alpha\right)\).
thought_text: A power function is not evaluated only at the null; it describes rejection probability across possible true parameter values.

question_text: For \(X_i\sim N(\mu,9^2)\), plan a two-sided level \(0.05\) test of \(H_0:\mu=0\) with at least 80% power when \(\mu=3\). Use the common approximation \(n\ge[(z_{\alpha/2}+z_\beta)\sigma/\delta]^2\), where \(\delta=|\mu-\mu_0|\) and \(\beta=0.20\).
solution_text: Here \(z_{\alpha/2}=z_{0.025}=1.96\), \(z_\beta=z_{0.20}=0.842\), \(\sigma=9\), and \(\delta=3\). Therefore \[n\ge\left(\frac{(1.96+0.842)9}{3}\right)^2=(8.406)^2=70.66.\] Round up to \(n=71\). The approximation is conservative-style for the common two-sided planning formula, and exact power could be checked using both tails.
thought_text: Sample sizes always round upward; otherwise the target power may not be met.

question_text: A test of \(H_0:\mu=10\) accepts \(H_0\) when \(8.5\le\bar X\le11.5\). Suppose \(X_i\sim N(\mu,4^2)\) and \(n=16\). Derive the operating characteristic \(L(\mu)=P_\mu(8.5\le\bar X\le11.5)\), and evaluate \(L(10)\) and \(L(12)\).
solution_text: The standard error is \(4/\sqrt{16}=1\). For any \(\mu\), \[L(\mu)=P_\mu(8.5\le\bar X\le11.5)=\Phi(11.5-\mu)-\Phi(8.5-\mu),\] where the arguments are in standard-error units because the standard error is 1. At \(\mu=10\), \(L(10)=\Phi(1.5)-\Phi(-1.5)=0.9332-0.0668=0.8664\), so \(\alpha=0.1336\). At \(\mu=12\), \(L(12)=\Phi(-0.5)-\Phi(-3.5)=0.3085-0.0002=0.3083\).
thought_text: The OC value at the null is the probability of non-rejection, not the probability of rejection.

question_text: For \(X_1,\ldots,X_n\sim N(\mu,\sigma^2)\) with known \(\sigma\), explain why the upper-tail Z test is a natural uniformly most powerful candidate for testing \(H_0:\mu\le\mu_0\) against \(H_1:\mu>\mu_0\).
solution_text: The joint normal likelihood depends on the data through \(\bar X\). For any simple comparison \(\mu_0\) versus \(\mu_1>\mu_0\), the likelihood ratio favoring \(\mu_1\) over \(\mu_0\) is an increasing function of \(\bar X\). By the Neyman-Pearson lemma, the most powerful level-\(\alpha\) test for that simple alternative rejects for large \(\bar X\). Because the same upper-tail ordering applies for every \(\mu_1>\mu_0\), the resulting upper-tail test is the natural uniformly most powerful test for the one-sided composite alternative, with the cutoff chosen at the boundary \(\mu=\mu_0\) to control the size over \(H_0:\mu\le\mu_0\).
thought_text: One-sided normal tests work cleanly because larger sample means consistently favor larger true means.

question_text: A report says: "We observed \(p=0.07\). Since this is not significant at 5%, the treatment has no effect. Also, because \(\alpha=0.05\), the chance that this conclusion is wrong is only 5%." Identify at least three problems with the report.
solution_text: First, failing to reject at 5% is not evidence that the treatment has exactly no effect; it means the data did not cross the pre-specified rejection threshold. Second, the chance that the conclusion is wrong is not \(\alpha\). The significance level is \(P(\text{reject }H_0\mid H_0\text{ true})\), not \(P(H_0\text{ false}\mid\text{fail to reject})\). Third, \(p=0.07\) may still be moderate evidence in a Fisher evidence sense, depending on context, even though it does not trigger a 5% Neyman-Pearson rejection. Fourth, the report says nothing about power or sample size; a non-significant result could be caused by low power.
thought_text: Final exams often reward diagnosing language as much as arithmetic.

question_text: Two tests use the same statistic for \(H_0:\theta=\theta_0\) against the same alternative. Test A has \(\alpha=0.01\) and power 0.55 at \(\theta=\theta_1\). Test B has \(\alpha=0.05\) and power 0.78 at \(\theta=\theta_1\). Which test is better? Give a decision-theory answer, not a one-word answer.
solution_text: There is no universally better test without specifying the relative costs of Type I and Type II errors or a constraint on \(\alpha\). Test A is more conservative: it has a smaller false-rejection probability under \(H_0\), but it misses \(\theta_1\) more often, with \(\beta=0.45\). Test B has larger Type I error probability but better detection at \(\theta_1\), with \(\beta=0.22\). If the design requirement is level 0.01, choose A or redesign. If the allowed level is 0.05 and detecting \(\theta_1\) is important, B may be preferred. Neyman-Pearson theory normally fixes an acceptable \(\alpha\) first and then compares power among tests satisfying that constraint.
thought_text: Higher power is attractive only among tests whose Type I error rate is acceptable for the problem.

question_text: For \(X_i\sim N(\mu,\sigma^2)\) with known \(\sigma\), consider the test of \(H_0:\mu\le\mu_0\) against \(H_1:\mu>\mu_0\) that rejects when \(\bar X>\mu_0+z_\alpha\sigma/\sqrt n\). Show that its size is \(\alpha\).
solution_text: The size is \(\sup_{\mu\le\mu_0}P_\mu(\text{reject})\). For this test, \[P_\mu(\text{reject})=P_\mu\left(\bar X>\mu_0+z_\alpha\frac{\sigma}{\sqrt n}\right)=1-\Phi\left(\frac{\mu_0-\mu}{\sigma/\sqrt n}+z_\alpha\right).\] When \(\mu\le\mu_0\), the term \((\mu_0-\mu)/(\sigma/\sqrt n)\ge0\), so this rejection probability is at most \(1-\Phi(z_\alpha)=\alpha\). The maximum occurs at the boundary \(\mu=\mu_0\). Therefore the size is \(\alpha\).
thought_text: For one-sided normal tests, the least favorable null value is the boundary closest to the alternative.

question_text: A manufacturer wants to detect whether mean battery life has dropped below 10 hours. Historical data justify known \(\sigma=1.2\). A sample of \(n=64\) batteries has \(\bar x=9.72\). The team asks for "a 95% confidence interval and a yes/no production decision." Diagnose the appropriate hypotheses, test direction, level if using a 95% one-sided decision standard, decision rule, observed decision, and how the confidence bound relates.
solution_text: The practical concern is a decrease, so use \(H_0:\mu\ge10\) or boundary \(H_0:\mu=10\) for calibration, against \(H_1:\mu<10\). A 95% one-sided decision standard corresponds to \(\alpha=0.05\). The standard error is \(1.2/\sqrt{64}=0.15\). The lower-tail rejection rule is \(\bar X<10-1.645(0.15)=9.753\). Since \(\bar x=9.72<9.753\), reject the no-drop null at level 0.05 and flag evidence that mean battery life is below 10 hours. The matching 95% upper confidence bound is \((-\infty,\bar x+1.645(0.15)] = (-\infty,9.967]\). Because the upper bound is below 10, it supports the same one-sided decision.
thought_text: For a less-than alternative, the matching confidence object is an upper bound; if that upper bound is below the threshold, reject.

question_text: For each mini-scenario, give the correct Neyman-Pearson diagnosis. A rule has \(\alpha=0.10\), but the observed \(p\)-value is 0.12. A test has power 0.92 at \(\mu=15\). A two-sided 99% confidence interval excludes \(\mu_0\). An engineer reduces \(\alpha\) without increasing \(n\). A one-sided alternative is \(\mu>\mu_0\), but the sample mean is far below \(\mu_0\).
solution_text: (i) Fail to reject at \(\alpha=0.10\), because \(p=0.12>0.10\). This does not prove \(H_0\). (ii) The Type II error probability at \(\mu=15\) is \(\beta(15)=0.08\). (iii) Reject \(H_0:\mu=\mu_0\) in the corresponding two-sided \(\alpha=0.01\) test. (iv) The critical region usually shrinks, so power usually decreases and \(\beta\) increases for fixed alternatives. (v) The observation is in the wrong direction for an upper-tail test, so it should not reject for \(H_1:\mu>\mu_0\), even if it is far from \(\mu_0\) in the lower direction.
thought_text: In final-style mixed questions, first identify the decision framework, then compute. Do not let a large numerical discrepancy override the stated alternative direction.
